questions.question: Explain pointer arithmetic with an example. Why is pointer arithmetic said to be "scaled"?
keyPoints: Pointer arithmetic adds/subtracts integer values scaled by sizeof pointed type
Example: int *p; p+1 adds 4 bytes on most systems
Scaling ensures pointer points to next element, not next byte
This allows seamless navigation through arrays of any data type

questions.question: What operations are allowed and not allowed in pointer arithmetic?
keyPoints: Allowed: pointer + integer, pointer - integer, pointer - pointer, pointer comparison
Not allowed: pointer + pointer, pointer * pointer, pointer / pointer
Only pointers to same array can be subtracted
Result of pointer subtraction is integer (number of elements between them)

questions.question: Explain the relationship between arrays and pointers in C with code example.
keyPoints: Array name acts as constant pointer to first element
arr[i] is equivalent to *(arr + i)
&arr[i] is equivalent to arr + i
Array name cannot be reassigned but pointer variable can
Both can be used for array traversal

questions.question: What is the difference between arr and &arr?
keyPoints: arr gives address of first element (type: int*)
&arr gives address of entire array (type: int(*)[5]) for array of 5 ints
Both have same numeric value but different types
&arr+1 skips entire array, arr+1 skips one element
This distinction is important for pointer arithmetic

questions.question: Explain NULL pointer and its significance in pointer operations.
keyPoints: NULL is defined as ((void*)0) representing invalid address
Dereferencing NULL leads to undefined behavior
Always check pointer is not NULL before dereferencing
Useful for initialization and error handling
Different from uninitialized pointer

questions.question: What is void pointer? Explain its limitations in pointer arithmetic.
keyPoints: void* can point to any data type
Cannot perform arithmetic directly on void pointer
Must cast to specific pointer type before arithmetic
sizeof(void) is undefined
Commonly used in memory functions like malloc, memcpy

questions.question: Explain the difference between *p++ and (*p)++ with examples.
keyPoints: *p++ increments pointer, returns original value (post-increment)
(*p) increments the value pointed to by p
*p++: p moves to next element, returns old value
(*p)++: p stays same, value at p increases by 1
These have different side effects and results

questions.question: Write a C program to reverse an array using pointer arithmetic only (without array indexing). Explain each step.
keyPoints: Use two pointers: start at first element, end at last element
Swap values using pointer dereferencing
Move start forward and end backward until they meet or cross
Continue until all pairs swapped
Time complexity O(n), space complexity O(1)
Demonstrates: pointer + integer, pointer - pointer, pointer comparison, pointer dereferencing

questions.question: Write a C program to search an element in a sorted array using binary search implemented with pointers. Explain pointer arithmetic usage.
keyPoints: Maintain low and high pointers (not indices)
Calculate mid pointer: low + (high-low)/2
Compare *mid with search key
Adjust low or high based on comparison
Continue until found or low > high
Demonstrates: pointer subtraction, pointer addition, pointer comparison

questions.question: Explain pointer arithmetic for different data types (char, int, float, double) with memory layout examples. Why is sizeof important?
keyPoints: char: +1 moves 1 byte, int: +1 moves 4 bytes (typically)
float: +1 moves 4 bytes, double: +1 moves 8 bytes
Memory layout shows contiguous storage of elements
sizeof determines the "step size" in arithmetic
Demonstrates why same pointer code works for arrays of any type
Scaling is fundamental to C's array-pointer relationship

questions.question: Write a C function that uses pointer arithmetic to find the length of a string without using strlen(). Explain the algorithm.
keyPoints: Start with pointer to first character
Increment pointer until null character encountered
Count increments (or use separate counter)
Return count
Uses: pointer initialization, pointer increment, pointer comparison, dereferencing
Time complexity O(n) where n is string length
